children.name: Taeniopygia
children.description: The zebra finches — a small genus of Australasian estrildids known for their distinctively patterned plumage and remarkable adaptability to arid environments. The Zebra Finch is one of the most studied songbirds in laboratory research, particularly in neurobiology, learning, and vocal communication. They are social, gregarious birds that form enormous flocks in suitable habitat.

children.name: Taeniopygia guttata
children.description: One of the most popular and well-studied cage birds in the world, the Zebra Finch is also abundant in the wild across most of Australia and the Lesser Sunda Islands. The male has a striking pattern of black-and-white 'zebra' stripes on the throat and chest, bright orange cheek patches, and a chestnut flank with white spots. It breeds opportunistically in response to rainfall, often raising multiple clutches in quick succession in the arid interior.

children.name: Padda
children.description: The Java sparrows — a small genus of stocky, large-billed estrildids native to islands of Southeast Asia. They are notable for their thick, conical bills adapted for husking rice and other grains. The Java Sparrow has been a popular cage bird in Asia for centuries, which has led to extensive introductions across the tropics. They are highly social and often roost in large communal groups.

children.name: Padda oryzivora
children.description: A striking, plump finch with a grey body, black head, white cheek patches, and a massive pink-red bill. It is native to Java, Bali, and Bawean in Indonesia but has been introduced widely across Asia, Africa, and the Pacific. Its fondness for rice earned it the scientific name oryzivora and made it a pest of agricultural importance, but its popularity as a cage bird has led to severe declines in its native range.

children.name: Erythrura
children.description: The parrotfinches — a genus of brightly coloured estrildids with green, red, and blue plumage that give them a parrot-like appearance. They are found from Southeast Asia to the Pacific islands, with the highest diversity in New Guinea. Their stout, curved bills are adapted for feeding on grass seeds, figs, and bamboo seeds. Many species are prized in aviculture.

children.name: Erythrura gouldiae
children.description: One of the most dazzlingly coloured of all small passerines, the Gouldian Finch occurs in three naturally occurring colour morphs: red-headed, black-headed, and yellow-headed. It is endemic to northern Australia, where it inhabits tropical savanna and woodlands, feeding almost exclusively on sorghum and other grass seeds. Wild populations declined severely in the 20th century due to altered fire regimes and habitat loss, but captive breeding programmes have been extremely successful.

children.name: Lonchura
children.description: The munias and mannikins — a large genus of seed-eating estrildids distributed across Africa, Asia, and Australasia. They are stout, typically brown or black seedeaters with conical bills, often with contrasting white or dark heads and bellies. Highly gregarious, they form noisy flocks in grasslands and rice paddies. Several species have been domesticated and are popular cage birds, including the Society Finch.

children.name: Lonchura domestica
children.description: A domesticated finch derived from the White-rumped Munia through centuries of selection in Japan and China. It is not found in the wild but exists only in captivity, where it is known for its gregarious, non-aggressive temperament and free-breeding habits. Its varied colour morphs include chocolate, pied, and fawn. It is often used as a foster parent for other estrildids in aviculture.

children.name: Lonchura punctulata
children.description: A small brown munia with a distinctive scaled pattern on the white breast and belly, giving it a snake-like appearance. It is native from India and Sri Lanka through Southeast Asia to the Philippines, Sulawesi, and northern Australia. It has been introduced to Hawaii, Puerto Rico, and parts of the Caribbean. It forms large, tight-knit flocks in rice paddies and grasslands, feeding heavily on grass seeds.

children.name: Amadina
children.description: The cut-throat and related finches — a small genus of African estrildids with stout cones and distinctive throat patterns. The Cut-throat Finch is named for the male's vivid red throat band that resembles a wound. These are birds of dry savannas and scrublands, often gathering at waterholes in large numbers. They build untidy grass nests in trees or bushes, often reusing old weaver nests.

children.name: Amadina fasciata
children.description: A distinctive finch of dry savannas across sub-Saharan Africa, the male sports a brilliant crimson band across the throat that looks like a bloody cut. The rest of the plumage is brown with fine barring on the upperparts and a finely spotted breast. It is a common bird of thorn scrub and grassy woodlands, often seen in flocks at waterholes, and is a popular aviary bird worldwide.

children.name: Amandava
children.description: The avadavats — a small genus of brilliantly plumaged Asian estrildids with striking red breeding plumage in males. They are among the most colourful small passerines in Asia, with the male Red Avadavat covered in tiny white spots set against a red background. They frequent tall grasslands and wetlands, building domed grass nests with a side entrance.

children.name: Amandava amandava
children.description: A tiny, brilliantly coloured finch in which the breeding male is crimson-red covered with small white spots, with red wings and tail. It is found across the Indian subcontinent to Southeast Asia, inhabiting grasslands, reedbeds, and rice paddies. Its high-pitched, tinkling song and undulating flight make it a noticeable presence in wet grasslands, and it is a popular cage bird in Asia.

children.name: Uraeginthus
children.description: The blue waxbills — a small genus of strikingly coloured African estrildids with brilliant blue faces, throats, and underparts. These delicate, fine-billed seedeaters are among the most beautiful African finches, occurring across savanna and woodland habitats. They are active, cheerful birds, often seen in pairs or small flocks at the edges of woodlands and in gardens.

children.name: Uraeginthus angolensis
children.description: A delightful little finch with a sky-blue face, throat, and breast contrasting with a pale belly and brownish upperparts. It is common across southern and central Africa in savanna, woodland edges, and gardens, often visiting waterholes in small parties. Its soft, high-pitched twittering calls and active, acrobatic movements make it a favourite among garden birdwatchers in South Africa.

children.name: Uraeginthus bengalus
children.description: A beautiful waxbill with sky-blue face and underparts, a brown back, and a red cheek patch in the male. It is widespread across sub-Saharan Africa in savanna and scrub habitats. Pairs are often seen at waterholes, and its soft, high-pitched calls and confiding nature make it a popular aviary bird.

children.name: Uraeginthus cyanocephalus
children.description: A striking waxbill with a blue crown and nape, blue breast and belly, and a warm brown back. It is found in East Africa from Somalia and Ethiopia to Tanzania, inhabiting dry savanna and thorn scrub. It is often seen in small flocks foraging on the ground for grass seeds.

children.name: Estrilda
children.description: The typical waxbills — a large genus of small, delicately proportioned estrildids found across Africa. They are named for the waxy red colouration of their bills, which resembles sealing wax. These active, social finches are predominantly fawn, grey, and brown with fine barring, and many show a distinctive red or black eye-stripe. They feed on grass seeds and small insects, often in large flocks at water sources.

children.name: Estrilda astrild
children.description: The most widespread and familiar waxbill in Africa, found from Senegal to Ethiopia and south to the Cape. It is a delicate, fawn-coloured finch with fine black barring, a red eye-stripe, and a characteristic bright red bill. It has been introduced to many islands worldwide and established feral populations in Portugal, Bermuda, Hawaii, and Brazil, where it thrives in grassy habitats.

children.name: Estrilda melpoda
children.description: A tiny, lively waxbill with a bright orange cheek patch, a red rump, and a pinkish-red bill. It ranges across West and Central Africa from Senegal to Angola and east to Uganda, inhabiting rank grasslands and marshy areas. It is a popular aviary bird and has been introduced to Puerto Rico, Bermuda, and parts of Japan, where small feral populations persist.

children.name: Estrilda troglodytes
children.description: A tiny waxbill of the Sahel and East African savannas, with pale fawn upperparts, a black rump, a red eye-stripe, and a pinkish-red bill. It is common in dry grasslands and thorn scrub, often in large flocks. It has been introduced to Hawaii and parts of Europe.

children.name: Estrilda rhodopyga
children.description: A small, slender waxbill with a bright crimson rump and upper tail, grey-brown upperparts, and a red eye-stripe. Found in East Africa from Sudan to Tanzania, inhabiting moist grasslands and marshes. It is often seen in small flocks, its crimson rump flashing conspicuously in flight.

children.name: Estrilda paludicola
children.description: A pale, fawn-coloured waxbill with a red rump, red eye-stripe, and a greyish breast. It is widespread in wetlands and moist savannas across Central and East Africa. Gregarious and active, it forms small flocks that feed on grass seeds in marshy areas.

children.name: Brunhilda
children.description: A small genus of African waxbills with distinctive black, white, and red facial patterns. They were formerly included in Estrilda but are distinguished by their heavier bills and striking black-and-red facial masks. They inhabit dry savanna and thorn scrub across eastern and southern Africa.

children.name: Brunhilda erythronotos
children.description: A striking waxbill with a black face and cheek patch, a red rump and belly, and fine barring on the upperparts. Found in dry savanna and thorn scrub from Angola and Namibia to Kenya. It is often seen in pairs or small parties, and its active, twittering behaviour makes it a conspicuous presence in arid landscapes.

children.name: Spermophaga
children.description: The bluebills — a small genus of large, robust estrildids with heavy blue-grey bills adapted for cracking hard seeds. They are found in forests and dense thickets across tropical Africa. Males are strikingly coloured with red, black, and white plumage, while females are duller. They are shy, retiring birds that forage on the forest floor.

children.name: Spermophaga ruficapilla
children.description: A large, striking finch with a bright red head and breast, black body, and a massive blue-grey bill. Found in Central and East African forests from Nigeria to Kenya and Tanzania. It is shy and secretive, foraging on the forest floor for fallen seeds and small fruits.

children.name: Mandingoa
children.description: A monotypic genus of African estrildid containing the Green Twinspot. It is a beautifully coloured finch of lowland and montane forests, with a glittering spotted breast that gives it its name. Its iridescent green spots are unique among African waxbills.

children.name: Mandingoa nitidula
children.description: A stunning little finch with a red face and throat, olive-green upperparts covered in iridescent green spots, and a black belly. Found in forests from Sierra Leone to Ethiopia and south to Zimbabwe and Mozambique. It is shy and difficult to observe, foraging quietly in the undergrowth for small seeds.

children.name: Lagonosticta
children.description: The firefinches — a genus of small, beautifully coloured African estrildids named for the brilliant crimson and pink plumage of the males. They are among the most vividly coloured small birds in Africa, with females being duller brownish versions. They inhabit savanna, scrub, and forest edges, often near water, and build domed grass nests with a side entrance.

children.name: Lagonosticta senegala
children.description: A tiny, brilliantly coloured finch with a red bill, red face and breast, and brown upperparts. The male is a vivid crimson-red; the female is brown with a red bill and rump. It is one of the most widespread firefinches, found across sub-Saharan Africa in savanna, gardens, and cultivated areas.

children.name: Lagonosticta rubricata
children.description: A striking firefinch with dark crimson upperparts, a red face and breast, and fine white spots on the flanks. Found from Senegal to Ethiopia and south to South Africa, inhabiting moist savanna, forest edges, and rank grasslands. It is shyer than the Red-billed Firefinch and prefers denser cover.

children.name: Lagonosticta rhodopareia
children.description: A small firefinch with a pinkish-red face and breast, brown upperparts, and a red rump. Found in East and southern Africa from Ethiopia to South Africa. It inhabits dry savanna, thorn scrub, and grassland, often near termite mounds, and is regularly seen in small family parties.

children.name: Granatina
children.description: A small genus of spectacular African estrildids noted for their brilliant violet, blue, and chestnut plumage. The Violet-eared Waxbill and Purple Grenadier are among the most beautiful of all African finches, with males displaying an extraordinary array of iridescent colours. They inhabit dry savanna and thorn scrub.

children.name: Granatina granatina
children.description: A stunning finch with a violet face and ear coverts, a chestnut crown and back, a blue throat and belly, and a red bill. Found in savanna and scrub from Angola and Zambia to South Africa. It is often seen in pairs, and its beauty makes it highly sought-after by birdwatchers visiting southern Africa.

children.name: Granatina ianthinogaster
children.description: A spectacular finch with a chestnut back, a brilliant violet-blue belly and rump, a red bill, and a long graduated tail. Found in East Africa from Somalia and Ethiopia to Tanzania. The male is one of the most vividly coloured of all estrildids, with the violet belly flashing conspicuously in flight.

children.name: Coccopygia
children.description: A small genus of African waxbills with yellow or greenish underparts and distinctive red rumps. They are among the smallest estrildids, with delicate bills and active, acrobatic feeding habits. They inhabit montane grasslands, forest edges, and cultivated areas in eastern and southern Africa.

children.name: Coccopygia quartinia
children.description: A tiny waxbill with olive-green upperparts, bright yellow underparts, a red rump, and a black bill. Found in East African highlands from Ethiopia to Tanzania and Malawi. Inhabits montane grasslands and forest edges, often in small flocks feeding on grass seeds.

children.name: Coccopygia melanotis
children.description: A tiny waxbill with a grey crown, olive back, yellow belly, red rump, and black ear patch. It is endemic to southern Africa, found in montane grasslands and coastal scrub from Zimbabwe to the Cape. Its high-pitched, sibilant 'swee-swee' call gives it its common name.
